data.title: InfuSystem: Expecting Strong Stock Gains In 2026
data.description:
This article is a continuation of coverage for InfuSystem (INFU), which I initiated in November 2025. You can refer back to that article for the company background information. The company's strategies have the potential to drive strong revenue and earnings growth in 2026 and probably beyond.

InfuSystem has been increasing margins, which is driving strong earnings growth. The valuation remains attractive, which increases the likelihood of strong stock gains. The stock also has a bullish technical setup, which suggests a possible continuation to the upside.

Growth Catalysts

INFU has multiple growth catalysts that can drive long-term top- and bottom-line growth. InfuSystem finished 2025 on a strong note with a 6.4% yoy gain in revenue to $143 million and a 19.3% yoy increase in operating cash flow to $24.4 million for the full year. The company also strengthened the balance sheet by achieving a 30% yoy decrease in net debt to $21.3 million. This performance shows that InfuSystem is on the right path for strong growth.

INFU's strong growth can continue due to the company's strategic initiatives. The company migrated its Wound Care business to the new revenue cycle application that was obtained as part of the Apollo Medical acquisition. The migration includes negative pressure wound therapy devices, advanced wound care, and INFU's newest product category - pneumatic compression devices. This move enables InfuSystem to reduce processing costs while expanding volume capacity, which can drive increased revenue. The company also plans to migrate its Oncology business (the last business left to migrate).

InfuSystem obtained new accreditations for additional home healthcare DME products that the company plans to add to its patient services product portfolio. INFU is working with some of the manufacturers of these products to achieve a quick launch.

The company did some restructuring of its biomedical services team to adjust for reduced volume expectations in 2026. This sets INFU up to add more smaller, profitable client engagements.

INFU also made progress on its project to replace & upgrade its main information technology business application. This is expected to be completed during Q1 2026. InfuSystem expects that the successful completion of this will reduce the spending rate for the project. Another expected benefit is to capture productivity improvements within several departments.

InfuSystem is projecting annual revenue growth of 6% to 8% for 2026. The company is also expecting its adjusted EBITDA margin to remain about the same in the mid- to low-20% range. The strong revenue growth should help drive INFU's stock higher as margins and earnings also increase.

Valuation

INFU is trading at 22x expected EPS of $0.42 for 2026. This improved from the November 2025 forward PE of 26x. However, it is lower than the sector median forward PE of 17x. What I said in my last INFU article also holds true now. What I said was that the one-year PEG ratio is a better valuation metric than the forward PE.

The PEG ratio takes the earnings growth rate into the equation. With the forward PE at 22x and the expected EPS growth rate at 34% for 2026, the one-year PEG is only 0.65. The stock is a bargain as the PEG is below one. INFU's high expected growth rate has the valuation at an attractive low level.

The 34% expected EPS growth can be achieved from INFU's expected revenue growth of 6% to 8% for 2026. The margin expansion that InfuSystem has been achieving can make up the difference. The gross margin increased from 52% to 56% from 2024 to 2025. The operating margin increased from 5.6% to 7.6%, and the net income margin increased from 1.7% to 4.6% over the same time. These strong margin gains can drive the bottom line growth, making it likely that INFU achieves analysts' consensus EPS estimates in 2026.

We can see in the chart above that INFU's EPS has been increasing over the past couple of years. At the same time, the stock price hasn't followed earnings and has remained stuck in a range. The stock tends to correlate with earnings growth over the long term. So, the current gap between the stock price and EPS could result in a stock rally as the price catches up.

Technical Perspective

InfuSystem's weekly chart above shows the stock possibly attempting to break higher from a bull flag formation. The bull flag's pole formed during August 2025, while the flag has formed since then. It looks like the stock formed another mini bull flag within the larger one in March and April of 2026. It is possible that this leads to a larger breakout above the $11 level, where the peak of the larger flagpole was in August 2025.

The RSI indicator is still bullish as it remains above the 50 level. The MACD is also bullish, as the blue line remains above the red signal line while the histogram bars turned green. I would like to see the MACD's histogram bars increase in length. This would show more positive momentum on strong stock price movement.

INFU's next earnings report, which is typically around the first or second week of May, could be the next catalyst for the stock. Analysts are expecting Q1 2026 revenue of about $34 million and GAAP EPS of $0.03. Positive results and upbeat forward guidance from the company could be the catalyst for the stock to break higher from the current bull flag formation.

Risks for the Stock

InfuSystem depends on favorable conditions for the healthcare reimbursement system. INFU depends on payments from government agencies and private health insurers for the use of its infusion equipment and related products. Reductions in these fees could result in revenue and earnings declines.

The potential for new therapies that deliver drugs in a more desirable way or ones with improved outcomes could be a risk for INFU. For example, if oral therapies were developed to replace infusions for the same drugs with effective results, it could lead to less demand for InfuSystem's products.

Competition is another risk for INFU. InfuSystem competes with other home care infusion providers, physician providers, and hospital-owned medical equipment suppliers. Increased competition in the form of more similar products in the market or newer products that perform better could lead to a market share loss for INFU and make it more challenging for the company to grow.

InfuSystem's Long-Term Outlook

InfuSystem has multiple growth strategies in place to drive long-term growth. INFU's above-average expected earnings growth could drive the stock for above-average gains in 2026 and possibly beyond. The company's margin expansion should help drive this strong expected earnings growth.

INFU's low valuation leaves plenty of room to the upside for the stock. The low valuation increases the chance for the stock to make above-average gains over the long term. The technical stock setup supports a continuation of the stock's recovery from the sharp drop in 2025.

data.title: Doubleview Gold Is Acquisition-Ready
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Green Vista Research as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Thesis

Doubleview Gold Corp. (DBG) (DBLVF) is trading at a significant mispricing. The company trades at a ~C$450M market cap, or roughly 0.06x P/NAV, which, in my view, cannot be reconciled with the underlying economics of the asset.

The discount likely exists for a few reasons. 1) The PEA models a C$3.6bn+ capital expenditure requirement, which is roughly 8x the company's market cap. There is no realistic path for the company to self-fund construction, which means the path to value likely comes from an acquisition or dilutive JV. 2) The Hat sits within Tahltan Nation territory, which creates significant permitting risk that the market is likely heavily incorporating into the share price. 3) Management has been historically slow to communicate and market the asset, which has kept institutional awareness of the resource low. I discuss each of these in this piece.

None of these factors, in my opinion, change the story of the Hat. The capex constraint is precisely what makes a DBG takeout the most likely outcome. A small company like DBG can't build out a mine with that level of required capital expenditure, but a major company can. And given the underlying economics and strategic importance of the minerals hosted by the deposit, DBG is an extremely attractive asset and likely to command a premium in the market.

Introduction

Doubleview Gold is a Vancouver-based mining exploration and development company that owns 100% of an extraordinarily unique deposit in North America - the Hat deposit. The Hat is a polymetallic deposit roughly 95 km southwest of Dease Lake in British Columbia's Golden Triangle, which is an area home to some of Canada's most significant mineral discoveries. This region has seen billions of dollars in development capital deployed over the past decade, and is still seeing investment today.

Doubleview was founded by current CEO Farshad Shirvani, who is also the company's largest shareholder. He owns 17% of the fully diluted share count.

The name Doubleview Gold is misleading. This is not a gold company. The Hat is a massive copper porphyry system that contains metals spanning copper, gold, cobalt, silver, and, one particular mineral we'll spend some time on later, scandium.

The company recently published a PEA for the Hat, which indicates that the fully-built project has:

  • After-tax NPV (5%) of C$7.27 billion at Consensus Metal Prices
  • After-tax NPV (5%) of C$14.85 billion at Spot Metal Prices

The current market cap is roughly C$450m, hence why I am writing this article. I believe this business is ripe for imminent buyout and, even after a MRE/PEA-driven surge in share price (followed by a rather heavy correction), is likely to command 4-9x its current share price given the implied economics of the Hat deposit.

Introduction to the Hat Deposit

The Hat is a copper-gold porphyry - the same geological class as some of BC's most significant mines: Red Chris (Newcrest/Newmont), Mount Milligan (Centerra), and Copper Mountain (Hudbay). The Hat is an alkalic porphyry, which usually implies open-pit operations that run for decades. Typically, porphyries are richer in gold relative to copper and can host unusual metals. In the Hat's case, those unusual metals include cobalt, silver, and scandium.

The sulphide footprint (the area where economically relevant mineralization has been found) spans more than 11 square km, which is large for a porphyry system. But the first key to understand from a valuation perspective is that only a fraction of that area has actually been drilled. The current resource (from the MRE 2.0) is based on a drilled area of roughly 2.6 square km, with only 97 drill holes. This means less than a quarter of the footprint has been drilled and modeled.

The 11 square km sulphide footprint means geologists have walked the surface, collected rock samples, and confirmed minerals are present across that area. But until they actually drill holes into it, they can't estimate grades or tonnage. They just know there's sulphide-bearing rock there. That's why the entire footprint is not included in the resource yet.

The PEA says that with just those 97 holes the project supports a measured and indicated resource of 609 Mt with another 503 Mt inferred. That is 1,112 Mt, which is monstrous for just 97 holes. The remaining ~8 sq km of sulphide footprint is all upside at this moment. It's obvious that this resource is likely to be worth more than what is currently modeled if more drilling is conducted.

The kicker to all of this is that many other deposits with similar tonnage have 400-700+ holes drilled. A few examples in the middle of that range:

  • Western Copper's Casino reported 464+ holes (~613 in the Yukon Minfile) in its 2022 FS.
  • Faraday's Copper Creek had 551 holes through its 2023 PEA.
  • Arizona Sonoran's Cactus reported 658 holes in its 2025 PFS.

These comparisons are illustrative rather than perfectly apples-to-apples comparisons with DBG. Obviously given DBG's unique qualities it is nearly impossible to find a precedent that is precisely comparable. And while these resources all vary in stage and nuance, my point remains. Most deposits need a multiple of the 97 holes that DBG has drilled to achieve a similar tonnage.

More drilling at the Hat would likely upgrade inferred resources to M&I and grow the total resource. NPV is very likely to increase in both cases. This matters because only M&I resources can be included in a PFS mine plan and currently 503 Mt of the Hat's 1,112 Mt resource is classified as inferred.

The deposit is still open in every direction.

PEA Economics

For reference, here is a link to the full PEA here.

DBG's PEA evaluates three scenarios:

  • Scenario A1 and A2 are the same operation: crush the ore, float it, then ship concentrate to a smelter.
    • A1 uses the recoveries from early lab tests (80% Cu, 66% Au)
    • A2 assumes the improvement you'd expect after optimizing the process (89% Cu, 75% Au)
  • Scenario B takes the base operation and bolts on a hydrometallurgical circuit that processes material that A1 and A2 would waste. This means Scenario B recovers additional gold, cobalt, and silver, plus scandium from the tailings. I summarize the assumptions here.

I like to think of Scenario A1 as the conservative case for reasons discussed below.

Sidenote: those who look at DBG's copper grade in isolation without considering the other metals will arrive at the wrong conclusion. The story of the Hat is its 0.42% CuEq grade, not the 0.19% Cu grade in isolation. A tonne of Hat ore at 0.19% Cu, plus gold, plus silver, plus cobalt generates roughly the same revenue as a tonne of ore at 0.42% Cu with no byproducts. CuEq captures the total recoverable value per tonne, which is what drives the NPV.

Deriving NPV

Step 1: Mining

To start, Doubleview has an open pit. They drill blast holes and blow up rock, then load it onto haul trucks and drive it somewhere. Every tonne of rock they move will cost money. Roughly C$2.32/t at the Hat according to the PEA:

The problem is that not all rock Doubleview moves will be actual ore. That's why the PEA gives a strip ratio (1.60:1), which is a metric that says for every tonne of ore sent to the mill, there will be 1.60 tonnes of waste. Waste costs the same to drill and blast and haul away, but doesn't actually generate revenue for the company. So the effective mining cost per tonne of ore is C$2.32 * (1 + 1.60) = C$6.03/t of ore, which you can see in the table above as well.

Step 2: Processing and Other Opex

Once the ore gets to the mill, Doubleview needs to get the metals out of the rock. At the Hat, the assumption the PEA makes is 120,000 tonnes per day get crushed, ground into powder, and then run through flotation. Flotation is where you add chemicals to ground-up rock and water. Air bubbles selectively carry the copper/gold/cobalt-bearing minerals to the surface as a froth. You skim off the froth. Then that concentrated froth is your copper concentrate (maybe 25-30% copper, plus gold and other metals). The leftover stuff (called the tailings) goes to a tailings pond.

Processing costs at the Hat are C$7.93/t milled. Then you have some additional G&A costs at C$2.56/t.

Total operating costs then come out to be C$16.22/t in the PEA.

Step 3: Production and Revenue

"Produced" is what the mill extracts from the ore. "Payable" is what the smelter pays DBG. The smelter keeps a percentage of the resources as part of their margin for refining the concentrate, which means DBG pays a few % of revenue directly to the smelter.

Under Scenario A1 we see annual revenue of approximately $1.2bn at consensus prices and $1.6bn at spot. This is gross payable revenue for the metals after the smelter's deductions.

Two things jump out from this table.

  1. Gold contributes US$501M against copper's US$664M. This is a supposed "copper" deposit where 41% of revenue comes from gold. That's why the CuEq grade (0.42%) is the right number to focus on, not the standalone copper grade (0.19%).
  2. Under A1/A2, 1,000t/year of Co would represent about 27% of total cobalt production in North America. From a single mine. The strategic value of domestic cobalt supply at a time when Western governments are actively trying to secure domestic resources bodes well for the Hat.

Step 4: EBITDA

There is one more layer of cost between gross revenue and what DBG actually receives.

  • C$77.00 of treatment charges
  • C$95.90 of transport & selling charges
  • Copper refining: C$0.11/lb
  • Gold refining: C$6.85/oz
  • Silver refining: C$0.48/oz
  • Cobalt refining: C$0.16/lb

These charges get deducted from the consensus and spot revenues from Step 3 to find EBITDA.

Step 5: NPV

Calculating NPV is then simple. I convert annual cash flow to a present value factor and then multiply post-tax FCF by that factor to get PV of the 25-year FCF stream. Then subtract capex and the PV of the closure. The reason the NPV from my calculation is smaller than the PEA NPV is because actual mines front-load the most mineral-heavy parts of the deposit. I'm using a constant cash flow model. This obviously impacts NPV quite dramatically given cash flow timing.

In actuality, the PEA NPV is more accurate because the Hat will produce more metal earlier in the mine's lifespan than what I have modeled. But this hopefully gives an idea of how the flow works in the PEA so we can talk valuation.

Conservative Valuation

As we've walked through the economics using Scenario A1, we will stay there to demonstrate value. In my opinion, A1 is the most conservative scenario. It uses just current test work recoveries, no scandium, no hydromet circuit, etc.

I also use my constant cash flow-derived NPV of $4.2bn CAD vs the $4.9bn CAD from the PEA to haircut even further. The PEA's NPV will be more accurate. Today, DBG trades at around 0.11x P/NPV ($4.2bn CAD).

If we sensitize the share price to NPV and implied multiples, we can see the implied share price across a range of NPVs and P/NAVs. The columns move from left to right across a range of metal price assumptions (all within Scenario A1): my derived (artificially low) consensus NAV (C$4.2bn) on the left, my derived spot NAV (C$9.9bn) on the right, with three interpolated midpoints between them. The rows represent the associated P/NAV multiples.

At 0.2x, which is still a somewhat conservative implied P/NAV multiple for a PEA-stage project, the implied share price ranges from C$3.54 to C$8.25 depending on the NPV used. And the current share price of C$2.02 falls below every cell shown in the table at every valuation except the single worst combination (0.1x on my again, artificially low, derived NPV). And even that number implies mitigated downside potential given the share price today.

The idea here is that even under artificially constrained scenarios, DBG should be trading at something like 2x what it is trading at today. That is - today - the share price should roughly double if the market were valuing it at a realistic, conservative number. Before we consider optimized recoveries, scandium, or spot metal prices, the share price should be 100% higher.

Let's now get to my base case. Scenario B.

Scenario B - Base Case

Scandium Introduction

You have likely never even heard of scandium. Which is fine, and makes sense because the global market is pretty small. Total global consumption is roughly 30-40 tonnes per year of scandium oxide (Sc2O3). China is the dominant producer, with additional supply from Russia, Kazakhstan, Ukraine, and the Philippines. And there is no exchange-traded market. Prices are generally negotiated privately between buyers and sellers.

There are two primary use cases for scandium:

  1. Aluminum-scandium alloys. Adding scandium to aluminum creates an alloy that is stronger, more weldable, and more corrosion-resistant than standard aluminum. Today, the applications include aerospace, defense, automotive lightweighting, and 3D printing of metal parts. The problem... is that manufacturers won't redesign components around aluminum-scandium alloys without a reliable, long-term supply at stable prices. And no reliable supply exists (until the Hat came along!), because without committed demand nobody builds a scandium mine. This is a classic chicken-or-the-egg problem.

  2. Solid oxide fuel cells. These are devices that convert hydrogen (or natural gas) directly into electricity. They use scandium as a key component. As the world invests more in hydrogen-powered energy, demand for scandium is likely to grow as well.

So yes, use cases for scandium exist, but you need a microscope to think through the economics. The Hat would produce 128 t/yr of Sc2O3 into a market that currently consumes 30-40 t/yr. Even if the chicken-or-egg problem resolves and demand grows meaningfully, the Hat alone would represent a multiple of current global consumption. The PEA's assumed price of US$1,500/kg exists in a scarcity environment, so whether that price holds at 3-4x current supply is an open question. Obviously, Scenario B economics are highly sensitive to the answer, and that's why the scandium isn't even included in A1/A2. The PEA's sensitivity analysis also shows that a 40% reduction in scandium price (to US$900/kg) reduces Scenario B NPV by approximately C$778 million.

And this is why I use Scenario A1 as the conservative case. A1 assumes zero scandium revenue, zero hydromet circuit, and just the current testwork recoveries. Even if the Hat works just as a copper-gold mine, it's a very strong investment. Scandium functions as the mine's call option, and that is why Scenario X (below) should be understood as more speculative.

Scandium at the Hat

Scandium grades have been formally estimated for 139 Mt (76 + 63 above) of the deposit's 1,112 Mt total resource. Total = 4,411 tonnes of Sc2O3. Keep in mind that this is just 139/1,112 = 12.5% of the total explored; the other 87.5% hasn't even been assessed for scandium yet. Do the math (hint: there is likely to be more scandium in the resource than what is shown in the PEA).

Remember from Step 2 above that flotation extracts copper, gold, silver, and some cobalt from the ore. The leftover tailings get sent to a storage facility. In Scenarios A1 and A2 the tailings are a waste. But in Scenario B, DBG takes 12.5% of those tailings (because that's all the PEA confirmed) and runs them through a hydrometallurgical circuit. This circuit uses acid generated from the mine's own pyrite to leach out scandium (and additional gold, cobalt, and silver that flotation missed). The scandium is refined on-site into Sc2O3 powder and then sold. It never goes to a copper smelter.

Economically, it's critical to understand that the ore has already been mined, crushed, and ground up. All of those costs are paid for by the base operation we went through in the prior section. The scandium circuit pays for just the cost of processing tailings that would otherwise be discarded.

Updated model with scandium is below. It includes the assumptions from the PEA to solve for the $7.2bn CAD NPV you see in the PEA headline.

Including the scandium makes this really interesting. Using the same 0.2x multiple with the $7.2bn CAD NPV, we can see that share price rises to approximately C$6. Again, this math uses only the numbers from current PEA findings. This is a 300% increase in share price at consensus pricing, and flexes up from there depending on how close you want to get to spot pricing. To be more explicit: a 0.2x P/NPV multiple used at spot pricing would be C$12.05, or a 600% increase from current pricing. This is pricing today, not implying any future growth of the resource. So 3x-6x from a base case perspective, assuming scandium assumptions hold.

Scenario X - Growth Case

The following scenario is my analysis and not part of the PEA.

There are a couple of things I've hinted at thus far that I've underplayed but matter greatly in thinking about the Hat.

  1. The entire PEA (the C$7.2 billion NPV, the billion-tonne resource, the 25-year mine life) is based on just 97 drill holes across 2.6 square km. The sulphide footprint spans 11 square km. Less than a quarter of the known mineralized area has been drilled, and every hole in the most recent season hit mineralization. As I've said prior - most deposits with comparable tonnage have been drilled with 400-700+ holes. The Hat achieved a billion-tonne resource with a fraction of the drilling of its peers. There is a lot of ground left to test.

  2. The PEA essentially disregards the scandium potential. If the pilot plant Doubleview is building demonstrates that scandium recovery works at a larger scale, that percentage increases and the NPV scales with it. The math is straightforward: Scenario B adds C$540M to NPV from processing just 12.5% of the tailings. If that relationship scales linearly and we move

    from 12.5% to 50%, the incremental NPV contribution grows to roughly C$2.1 billion. If we try to quantify this further:

  • The current resource sits at 1,112 Mt / 2.6 sq km = 428 Mt per sq km of drilled area. If the undrilled ground yields just 25% (conservative) of that density, the resource grows to roughly 2,000 Mt
    • 428 * 0.25 = 107 Mt per sq km
    • 107 * 8.4 sq km of undrilled footprint = 899 Mt
    • 1,112 + 899 = 2,011 Mt.

However, a larger resource doesn't translate directly to a proportionally larger NPV because of the heavy discounting applied to outyear cash flows. But that misses the more immediate value driver from additional drilling, which is that the inferred resources are likely to be upgraded to M&I, which increases confidence in the current economics and allows those resources to carry into a PFS.

Combining both levers (scandium scaling and resource growth), we arrive at Scenario X:

Component Consensus NPV

Basis

Scenario B

 =C$7,270m Optimized recoveries and Sc at 12.5%
+ Scandium scaled to 50% of tailings (assumption) +C$1,620m Processing more scandium
+ 25% NPV uplift from drilling (assumption) +C$1,818m Resource growth + inferred leading to higher M&I
Scenario X =C$10,700m


These are also using consensus prices! I'm not using spot prices for any of the assumptions above. I also don't assume more than 50% of the tailings are processed for scandium. And I assume the undrilled ~8 sq km yields only 25% of the resource density seen in the drilled area, which is deliberately conservative relative to what the drilling results to date would suggest.

At today's C$2.00 ish share price, the market is pricing DBG at roughly 0.04x P/NAV using Scenario X. At the conservative 0.20x multiple used in the base and conservative cases, the implied share price would rise to C$8.92. This is, again, at consensus metals pricing. Spot pricing levers this hard and shows much more upside. No assumptions I have included so far push the envelope to the point where a share price like that is unrealistic.

Recent Precedent Transaction

During March 2026, Hudbay Minerals acquired Arizona Sonoran for ~US$1.48B, roughly 64% of ASCU's PFS NPV. ASCU, however, was one study stage more advanced than the Hat (PFS vs PEA), so it was further de-risked and likely to command a higher multiple. Here's how the Cactus project compares to the Hat:

Metric Cactus The Hat
M&I Resource 11 bn lbs Cu 5.82 bn lbs CuEq

Inferred Resource

 1.7 bn lbs Cu 4.57 bn lbs CuEq
Total Resource 12.7 bn lbs Cu 10.39 bn lbs CuEq
Grade 0.48% (all Cu) 0.42% CuEq
Study Stage PFS PEA
NPV (after-tax) US$2.3 bn (NPV8) US$5.3 bn (NPV5, Scenario B)
Acquisition Price US$1.48 bn

N/A
P/NAV Paid 0.64x

Trading: 0.06x


Note that ASCU's NPV uses an 8% discount rate while the Hat uses 5%. A higher discount rate produces a lower NPV, so the comparison is not perfectly apples-to-apples. My NPV derives an A1 NPV of C$4.25 billion at a 5% discount rate, which captures approximately 85% of the PEA's reported C$4.96 billion (the gap reflecting the PEA's front-loaded ore scheduling, which my model does not replicate). At an 8% discount rate, the model produces an A1 NPV of approximately C$2.4 billion. Adjusting upward by the same 85% ratio to approximate what the PEA would report at 8%, the estimated NPV8 is approximately C$2.8 billion.

Also, the Hat's PEA uses a consensus copper price of US$4.88/lb versus ASCU's PFS at US$4.25/lb. Part of the NPV gap reflects this higher price assumption.

A few other things to understand from this acquisition:

  1. The total resource is comparable. Cactus has more M&I (11 vs 5.82 billion lbs), but the Hat has far more inferred (4.6 vs 1.7 billion lbs) with a high likelihood of growth in both of those numbers. Total resources are in the same ballpark: 12.7 vs 10.4 billion lbs. Cactus's higher M&I reflects more advanced drilling (658 holes versus the Hat's 97 - remember what I said earlier about the drilling gap?). The Hat will likely close this gap as drilling continues and inferred material gets upgraded.

  2. After normalizing both projects to NPV8, the discount gap narrows from roughly 10x to roughly 4x, but 4x is still a large valuation discrepancy.

Here's an exercise: what would the Hat be worth at ASCU's multiple? After normalizing the Hat's NPV to use a similar 8% discount rate:

At today's price of ~C$2.00, the implied upside at ASCU's 0.64x multiple ranges from roughly 4x (Scenario A1 at NPV8) to roughly 9x (Scenario X at NPV8). Again, these scenarios all use consensus metals pricing - there is zero upside baked into the calculations regarding spot prices. Using spot prices puts those implied share prices even higher...

Risks

Unfortunately, with high potential reward comes high risk. And the largest risk to this project is that the Hat sits within Tahltan territory on land the Nation considers culturally significant.

Doubleview's relationship with the Tahltan has been shaky. A brief timeline of interaction:

  • In 2015, Tahltan members visited the Hat drill site and asked workers to stop. Workers agreed and left. DBG's CEO (Shirvani) responded by calling the RCMP and seeking a court injunction against the Elders and Tahltan government leaders.
  • In March 2021, the Tahltan Central Government formally announced its opposition to DBG's continued operations, resulting in a trade halt. The two parties subsequently signed a Communications and Engagement Agreement in July 2021, under which DBG publicly apologized and agreed not to enforce the injunction.
  • In July 2023, the TCG restated its opposition, citing unresolved conflicts, and called on the Province to deny DBG any further permits within Tahltan Territory.

To be clear: there is still unresolved risk. If the Tahltan Nation decides this mine should not be developed, the resource is likely worth nothing. That said, the Tahltan have productive relationships with every other major operator in the Golden Triangle including Newcrest/Newmont at Red Chris, Skeena at Eskay Creek, Newmont/Teck at Galore Creek, and have never permanently blocked a metals mine in their territory. Given Doubleview's history with the Tahltan, however, I see the risk as material.

The most likely path to resolution is that whoever wants to acquire the Hat negotiates an agreement directly with Tahltan leadership. But that outcome is speculative. Investors should weight this risk heavily. It is, in my view, the primary reason DBG trades at such a steep discount to its intrinsic value, and it is not unreasonable for the market to discount it as aggressively as it does.

The other "risk" - or rather, "pain", is that management moves slowly. Shareholders such as myself have experienced many delays over time (sometimes months on end) when it comes to reporting. Both the MRE 2.0 and the PEA were originally expected much earlier than they were delivered. The delays, though, are less concerning given Shirvani's ownership in the resource. His wealth is tied to the outcome of the project. However, it can be difficult to invest in a company with such opacity and lack of communication. Their social media accounts can read rather "retail-y" (excessive emoji use), which I don't like, because the resource is so incredible and for some reason they don't market it in investor-friendly terms. Which, if you are a diligence maniac, can actually be a good thing as the market remains unaware of a resource that's worth multiples of what it should be.

Other risks are more what you'd expect based on the PEA:

High initial capex: The PEA models initial capex of C$3.6-3.8 billion against a market cap of ~C$450 million. Doubleview can't self-fund construction, so the path to production requires an acquisition or a JV that would dilute existing shareholders. Metal price sensitivity is also significant.

Uncertainty around the scandium and its end markets: The PEA models 128 tonnes/yr of Sc2O3 to be sold into a very small market. Scenario X becomes overly optimistic if the scandium can't find buyers at the PEA's assumed price.

Dilution: The company will continue raising capital for the pilot plant, additional drilling, PFS-stage engineering, etc. So naturally shareholders like myself will see some dilution.

Conclusion

The Hat is, in my opinion, the most compelling risk-reward opportunity in the mining sector today. And I don't see many more opportunities like this across the broader market, especially at a time when governments are hunting strategic metals amidst geopolitical uncertainty.

The TLDR: DBG's market cap is C$450m for a resource conservatively worth C$5-7bn+ at consensus metals pricing. Expansion of the resource is highly likely, as only a fraction of the total footprint has actually been drilled. While DBG trades at 0.06x P/NAV, a comparable project just traded at 0.64x P/NAV, which, on a normalized basis, is still 4x+ higher than what is currently assigned to the Hat under conservative assumptions. The Hat is located in a Tier 1 jurisdiction, open in every direction, and sitting on a call option in the form of scandium. There have only been 97 holes drilled, which is a fraction of what most companies need to drill in order to achieve a similar tonnage.

Either the market is right and there is a fatal flaw I'm not seeing, or nobody is aware just how extraordinary this resource actually is. If I were a major mining company, I would be actively evaluating DBG as a target. I feel confident that as we sit here today, the stock is worth 3-5x what it is trading at under conservative assumptions, and I'm a believer that its eventual takeout is on the horizon, and when it happens, price will be even higher than the 5x it should be trading at today.

I have been long DBG since early 2023. This is not investment advice. Investing in this business means accepting high risk.

The world needs more resources like the Hat. Let's hope they execute and make some money in the process.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Exploring Digital Equity With These 3 Stocks
data.description:
Digital credit has been a big topic lately. It's a major tool of digital asset treasuries ("DATs"), companies that build a "treasury" of a cryptocurrency. I wanted to tackle the topic head-on today. So I'll talk about major DATs: Strategy (MSTR), DeFi Development Corp (DFDV), and Sharplink (SBET), and I'll say which ones I prefer for investment.

Basic logic of digital equity

Why buy shares of a DAT when you could just buy the coin itself or an ETF? It's because DATs give levered returns with less risk. This comes from being C-corps. It lets them use long-term financing to acquire crypto without margin calls.

They can issue bonds or preferred stock and raise capital. They can purchase their target coin with itl. If the coin's appreciation is enough to cover dividends or interest, common shareholders benefit.

A DAT's common stock is often more volatile than crypto. So the stock could be at premium value to holdings. If so, the DAT sells more, and they get the premium value in cash. They can use this to buy more crypto or pay expenses without selling their holdings.

DATs often have a dashboard on their website showing their stock's "mNAV." This is the multiple to their crypto's liquidation value. If the mNAV is higher than 1x, then it's at a premium and can be sold favorably.

Most DATs try to grow their coin per share (BTC per share, SOL per share) by taking advantage of this flexible financing.

Different coins, different DATs

Which cryptocurrency a DAT chooses for its treasury depends on its prospects. The DATs I mentioned before have all chosen different coins. MSTR does Bitcoin (BTC-USD), DFDV does SOL (SOL-USD), and SBET does Ether (ETH-USD).

I think a BTC DAT is the simplest. There's no operating business to it. They issue securities to raise capital and buy BTC. They can limit total debt or preferred equity used. Then they hope BTC appreciates enough to work out. If you think BTC will do well over time, you should think a BTC DAT like MSTR will do better.

With ETH and SOL, it's different. Those DATs have treasury operations. This mostly comes from staking. You can stake ETH. You can stake SOL. They have uses on their native blockchains. These chains enable smart contracts. That's why they can be deployed to earn yield. BTC can't do this.

Operational DATs can pay their dividend or interest expenses with this yield. That's a big advantage. It's why I can't bring myself to rate MSTR better than Hold. It doesn't do anything with its BTC treasury.

So then it's a question of which blockchain is better for a DAT.

Onchain finance and DATs

Really, that depends on what happens with finance. Many think that AI agents will play a role here. Management of both DFDV and SBET concur with this. On top of that, tokenization of real-world assets creates more use cases for smart contracts. Put these together, and both blockchains become more useful. This creates demand for ETH or SOL.

DFDV was the first public DAT to go outside of BTC, and their cryptocurrency of choice was SOL. Of the major blockchains, they also think Solana is best suited to scale up for the trends ahead.

In their case for DFDV, they point to Solana's lead in total transactions for 2025 as a sign. Securitize, Inc. (CEPT) is a leader in tokenizing assets onchain. They have endorsed Solana as having a big role to play.

Ethereum's advantage is that they were the first mover. They created the first infrastructure for smart contracts.

Because of that, more money is behind ETH. Its market cap is $270.6B, while SOL's is $48.6B. This also means that many more developers operate there.

So it's SOL's speed and low costs versus ETH's incumbency and network effect. These tailwinds could go either way over time.

Both are buys

It's tempting to say that one stock is better in a comparison, but I think there is a case for both DFDV and SBET.

Both trade at a discount to mNAV, an indicator of undervaluation for a DAT. There aren't earnings in a traditional sense to value here. DATs are sort of like dynamic asset plays. They enhance their assets with their operations, plus natural tailwinds.

If there's a rule of thumb to ease up on the buying, I'd say it's when the shares reach 1.2x NAV.

Both companies have relatively healthy balance sheets. DFDV's is leveraged, and SBET remains free of any debt or preferred equity. That might be why DFDV's mNAV is lower. It has costs of capital.

Risk Factors

There is a problem with the discounts too. It's impossible to issue common stock favorably. Issuing DFDV or SBET would be dilutive relative to SOL or ETH acquired. They'll have to use more leverage or just wait for their coins to recover since the crypto bearishness that kicked off in the fall. This rut for crypto occurred in the aftermath of the October 10 liquidations.

DFDV disclosed in their shareholder letter that the liquidations made their normal staking strategy temporarily unprofitable and led to somewhat weaker Q4. Disruption in either ecosystem will disrupt operations. It's not just a financing risk. Operations are affected too.

Final thoughts

Digital equity is a viable way to invest in crypto. But it does take a discerning eye. I can't say BTC will fail, but I also can't see why it should succeed like SOL or ETH over time. If there are paradigm shifts with AI and tokenization, I'd want to be behind the right infrastructure. That is where digital equity has a chance at being something.

data.title: DaVita: Further Upside Is Trickier (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
My investment in DaVita (DVA) has been a largely successful one so far since I started pushing it more at the end of 2025, and again in February of 2026. You can find my last article on the company here, and the resulting gain in my (unfortunately, relatively small) position is about 24%, inclusive of FX. Given that the market is down in the same timeframe, though, I'm pretty happy with my return on the investment.

Could the return be forecasted? I'm talking, obviously, about the significant bounce in share price that we saw in early 2026, about two months back. In one way, I say yes - in the other way/perspective, no. I say yes because, as I laid out in my previous piece, DaVita was undervalued for what it was, even with a negative RoR. On the other hand, no one could have precisely predicted the 4Q and the market reaction that the quarter brought. That's the trick with value investing. You may be aware that a company is trading cheaply, or at a low value relative to its "fair" value - but you can never really expect when the company goes up or when it does not. Sometimes, you can be stuck in an investment for years without it moving the needle, despite all signs pointing to the fact that it "should". Then, one day, it's almost as though the market "wakes up" and starts increasing the company's value.

For me, knowing that I am investing in assets at a discount that I consider to be appealing is enough. Time for me, as in the time I need to wait for an investment to go up, is largely irrelevant, as long as I believe it will. This is eased further by the fact that I usually get an attractive dividend out of my investment, which "sweetens the pot" while I'm waiting for the company to mature.

In this article, I'll be looking at the latest results and determining if there's further upside to the company after a short-term return, which can amount to a triple-digit RoR on an annualized basis.

DaVita - Upside in dialysis may continue into 2026-2027.

So, on a general level, and depending on how you value the company, yes. DaVita may continue upward. But the details of it are a bit more complex than this.

As I highlighted in my previous piece, the decline to less than $110/share market is a substantial discount to any sort of average in the company. At its lowest point, DaVita traded at less than 10x P/E. That is a substantial, near-40% discount to a company that usually trades at a 20-year average of 16x P/E and, over time, has managed to grow its earnings by 13% per year, and is currently estimated to grow them on average 18% the next 3 years (that's higher than the growth average). While there is a considerable amount of volatility in the company's forecasts historically, much of that volatility tilts towards the positive side. What I mean by this is that DaVita doesn't hit targets, but it often beats them.

The above illustration provides a snapshot of what the market expects of DaVita, relative to what it has typically been able to achieve. Positives for the company, much like Fresenius (FSNUY) are that it is an inherently profitable business with strong annual cash flow generation. Downsides, from an extremely high level, are that growth is stagnant - that is the same with its peers - and that DaVita, generally speaking and from a comparative perspective, is not the most foundationally safe business.

This is reflected in two ways. First, the company is BB rated. This is sub-par, but especially for Fresenius, it is significantly lower. However, the biggest reflection of the company's subpar safety and fundamentals is reflected by delving into its balance sheet. Doing so, you find that the company has high leverage - over $9B in debt and over $11B in total liabilities. So while profitable, this company's balance sheet is not in the best shape, and debt/EBITDA is about 3.1x, which could inflate interest costs and other factors. These are things to consider.

There are things to consider, especially because DaVita does not pay a dividend. So that sweetened pot I spoke about, that's not there. You're investing for capital appreciation. Based on the company's history, I consider it very unlikely that any dividend is forthcoming with this company - and this is something you need to consider. That's also, tying back to my previous piece, why I consider the 20-year average P/E of 16x to be entirely too optimistic. I can get better returns with these considerations.

So, the primary issue with DaVita is, I would argue, its debt relative to its ability to grow earnings. The 2025 results are important for this reason. The results provided a beat in EPS, a top-line revenue growth of almost 10%, and more of the company's shift towards what is considered value-based care.

The company's IKC platform finally achieved its first year of operating profitability, which is ahead of the company's original target (2026 originally), which is probably the core reason for the reversal that we saw in conjunction with the earnings report. What also affected or led to the company's climb in valuation was a considerable amount of share buybacks. With regards to this, I am torn.

On the one hand, the company was objectively cheap at the time of the buybacks. On the other hand, a company laden with the sort of debt that the company is should not prioritize mechanical increases of earnings through buybacks, but lower its debt. That $1.8B for buybacks in 2025 alone could have cut the debt below $8B, and this would have, in my opinion, been at least something to consider. The company's net debt/FCF is now above 7.3x, and the proof of rising interest expenses is, as they say, in "the pudding", with interest costs rising over 45% YoY in 2Q25. I expect the trend to continue. From filings, debt at 3.75%-4.5% is now up to 6.75%.

The company responded in earnings calls that the model of the business is one of strong, high cash flows, and the debt is therefore manageable. I respect the argument, but I disagree with the sentiment on the basis that it exposes the company to volatility (interest) that, based on this response, the company chooses to have.

So while the company had 2025 results that were, on an objective basis, very good, I continue to have problems with how the company uses its capital. It's my view that the company prioritizes short-term shareholder trends above longer-term capital appreciation, safety, and company development. And this is a problem.

That, however, doesn't mean that the company is no longer cheap.

Valuation for DaVita - upside is tricky, but still there

In valuing DaVita, two assumptions are core to the future potential of the company and to the company's upside from here. First, at what growth rate is the company's AEPS likely to grow? While recent results argue for revenue growth potential, I argue that this is not from volume itself, but from pricing and business model, because volume growth in kidney care is difficult and has been for some time. This is due to significant mortality rates, the shift to home-based care (which impacts volumes and margins), and very tricky regulatory foundations. It can also be seen, however, by treatment growth not meeting the company's internal targets of 1-3% growth.

Here are the issues with what I see with EPS growth. Much of it is expected to come from continued share buybacks. DaVita has grown its EPS significantly by a considerable amount due to a reduction in SO as of late. For years, it has reduced the number of shares outstanding by over 50%.

Many of these buybacks were not done at what I would consider inherently attractive pricing, looking at the valuation trends since 2019. I therefore view significant amounts of the company's expected EPS growth as artificial. This is part of what drives my PT to relatively low levels. In my last article, I put it at $130/share - which would mean that the company is a "HOLD" now, unless I raise my PT by $20/share.

So do i?

Based on how the company allocates capital and how it plans for the next few years, I'm very comfortable not increasing my price target here. My PT is, as I can see from other analyst averages, one of the lowest out there. I work with a current P/E of less than 11x P/E, which is more than a 35% discount from the 20-year average.

But given that much of the company's estimated AEPS growth is entirely based on things like buybacks, I do not believe this to be in any way illogical. The upside to my 11x P/E is less than 15% annualized. This is mostly due to the lack of a dividend, because estimated growth is actually good (again, though, we have a lot of buybacks).

It is to be noted that DaVita is currently under an agreement with Berkshire (BRK.A) (BRK.B) for the latter to maintain its stake at below 45%. This requires BRK.B to continue to sell off shares as DaVita continues to buy back more, which puts a bit more pressure on the share price (or negatives the effects of the buybacks, however you want to interpret it).

The dividend lack, the buybacks, and the BB-credit (which, to be clear, is a junk rating) are what drive me to not increase my PT. In my last article, I went through many of the operational risks and difficulties, such as reimbursement. These are not gone, they are the other pieces of the puzzle and are still highly relevant. In this article, I add to this risk the risk for EPS growth being largely related to the mechanical effects of buybacks, the other part that moderates my PT for the company.

I reiterate $130/share - the current upside was always part of my thesis. I may rotate the company here.

Risks and Considerations

The risks for DaVita relative to its forecast are, as I mentioned, primarily related to Medicare reimbursement. Most of the other risk factors fade, in my view, when the company's relative business volume and the risks a cut would entail are put in context. In contrast to other analysts, I consider the risk for revolutionary new drugs or treatments (including things like more regenerative medicine) as something speculative, and not something to directly impact the valuation. I retain this view as of 2026, and current indications or coming products for the market in 2026 do not change my view.

Even with the coming and integration of at-home care, this would hardly negate the company's advantage in terms of intangible assets and its center network, though it may certainly impact its profitability - but I believe the need for the services will remain regardless. With the company's buyback trends diluting the growth upside, I believe my PT is moderated enough here.

Thesis

DaVita is an attractive company at the right price. The company's fundamentals, including its credit rating, lack of a yield, and historical volatility, together with its buyback trends, represent an investment hurdle requiring careful consideration as to how we should set the bar for valuation for the company.

I reiterate the lowest 20-year average available for the company, which comes to a 12-13x P/E as an upside, with a PT around 11x P/E. At 11-12x P/E and a double-digit rate of growth, we can see a fair value for the company coming to 17-25% per year. However, because much of that upside is related to buyback as the company has done in the recent past, I do not shift my target from my last article.

I reiterate a PT of about $130/share, even after the upswing. That makes the company a "HOLD". It should be noted, however, that there's a fair bit of volatility here, which is why I am considering rotating.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is qualitative
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversions.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of $130/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's not attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of April 2026.

data.title: The Only Dividend Strategy I'd Trust In A 3.5% Fed Funds World
data.description:
When investing in high-yielding stocks, one of the biggest considerations to keep in mind is the direction of interest rates moving forward. This is because many high-yield stocks are bond proxies, such as REITs (VNQ) like Realty Income (O) or preferred ETFs like the Virtus InfraCap U.S. Preferred Stock ETF (PFFA), or even infrastructure stocks like Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP)(BIPC). When interest rates fall, these investments tend to soar higher, and when interest rates rise, they tend to slump.

Meanwhile, business development companies (BDCs) (BIZD) are often the opposite. Whenever the Fed enters a rate-hiking cycle, BDCs tend to move higher, whereas when the Fed cuts rates, BDCs tend to experience dividend cuts and see their stocks fall out of favor because they invest in floating-rate loans primarily. However, my personal view and that of the market at the moment is that interest rates are likely to remain in a fairly narrow band for the foreseeable future. In this article, I will detail why and share how I am investing as a result.

Rates Likely Staying Put

The Federal Reserve has held the Fed funds rate steady at between 3.5% and 3.75% for two consecutive meetings to start 2026. The dot plot and CME's FedWatch currently project between zero and one rate cuts as the most likely scenario this year, with perhaps one more in 2027. This means that for the foreseeable future, the market expects the federal funds rate to remain around 3.5%.

What this means is that investors who are betting heavily on capital appreciation from falling interest rates by piling into REITs, utilities (XLU), preferreds (PFF), and other bond proxies are likely to be disappointed. At the same time, investors in sectors like BDCs who are hoping for a rebound in net investment income from rising rates are also likely to be disappointed. Thus, in this sort of environment, value investing and opportunistic capital recycling are going to be increasingly important for driving total returns rather than simply making a successful macro call on where interest rates are headed.

My Core Investment Strategy

So, how am I investing with this in mind? First of all, my core fundamental approach to investing remains the same. In fact, I'm putting even more emphasis on it now, namely buying high-quality businesses that pay high yields with a margin of safety built into the valuation across seven core sectors:

  • Midstream MLPs (AMLP)

  • BDCs

  • REITs

  • Alternative asset managers

  • Infrastructure

  • Energy producers (XLE)

  • Precious metals-related investments (GDX)

Beyond that, I'm also finding opportunities in industrials (XLI) and consumer goods stocks, and even a few technology stocks have emerged that pay out high yields. Once I build out my well-diversified portfolio of quality dividend payers on attractive valuations, I then sit back patiently and collect dividend income while I wait for Mr. Market to recognize the embedded value in my investments. Once he begins to wake up to those, I trim and potentially even sell those positions as they approach fair value and recycle the capital into even more attractive opportunities, to not only potentially accelerate the total return compounding process, but also to accelerate dividend growth in my portfolio, as I tend to recycle the capital into other positions that pay out even higher dividend yields since they also trade at cheaper valuations. In fact, it is this very strategy that has enabled me to deliver total return outperformance and, at the same time, generate a 7–8% annualized yield in my portfolios since launching them a little over five years ago.

This strategy is one that I expect to work especially well in a stable rate environment because valuations will be even easier to judge as being too high, just right, or too low. One of the chief uncertainties in setting an accurate valuation, namely, interest rates, should be more stable. The reason why this strategy works so well is that passively managed ETFs like the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) and, to a lesser extent, the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) generally engage in systematic rebalancing, typically once a year. While this does work well in driving dividend growth for SCHD based on how it's structured, the once-a-year programmatic rebalancing is not as opportunistic as my approach. It is also rule-based and therefore unable to account for nuances that an active investor like me can take into account.

Midstream MLPs: Rate-Independent Compounders

So, with this in view, how am I investing in each of the seven core sectors that I mentioned previously?

Midstream MLPs are relatively rate-independent because they generate very stable, inflation-like cash flows. This means that when inflation is elevated, which generally leads to rising rates, their cash flows often increase due to a combination of rising energy prices (since energy is a fairly good inflation hedge) and their inflation-linked contracts also tending to boost their cash flows. Meanwhile, when rates fall with inflation being low, sure, energy prices often drop, as do their contract escalators, but the bond-like nature of their contracted cash flow generally offsets that in terms of the market's perception of the intrinsic value of the asset.

Therefore, my approach here is the same as always, and while the surge in energy prices to start the year has led to a strong rally in the midstream space, I'm still finding some attractive opportunities, including names like Energy Transfer (ET) and Western Midstream Partners (WES), as I recently detailed here.

BDCs: A Sweet Spot Emerging

In the BDC space, I think we are about to see a net investment income per share stabilization because spreads, which had been compressing, seem to have bottomed due to capital slowing into the space amid concerns about private credit, as well as the fact that the Fed is likely to pause cutting rates for a while, which means that the floating-rate loans will not see their base rates decline further for a while either.

This creates a sweet spot for BDC income, since base rates are still fairly high relative to where they have been over much of private credit's history, and the economy remains in sound shape, especially for senior secured loans. When you combine that with the very attractive valuations available in the space in blue-chip names like Hercules Capital Corporation (HTGC), Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL), Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC), and others, now is a great time to put money to work in the BDC space, in my view, especially given that non-accrual rates remain quite low.

REITs: Selective Opportunities in Multi-Family

Meanwhile, in the REIT sector, you have to be really selective because REITs tend to only experience dramatic upside in most cases when interest rates shift dynamically, as they generally do not grow very much, and the ones that do grow a lot tend not to yield very much. Therefore, I'm being very selective and looking for blue-chip names that are materially undervalued relative to their private market value, have stable cash flows, attractive dividend yields, and potential upside catalysts.

One place where I'm seeing a lot of this is in the multi-family space with names like Mid-America Apartment Communities (MAA), Camden Property Trust (CPT), and several others. I expect improving supply and demand dynamics, especially in some Sun Belt markets, in the coming years to boost these REITs materially higher as they close their discounts to private market values, pay out dividends with ~5% yields, and grow at a solid clip as well.

Alternative Asset Managers and Infrastructure

Meanwhile, in the alternative asset management space, picks are plentiful, including Blackstone (BX), Ares Management (ARES), Blue Owl Capital (OWL), and several others. I recently released a deep dive into why I think OWL is a particularly attractive buy right now. However, all these names look very undervalued to me, since they're being put in the penalty box for their private credit exposure, even though their underlying private credit fundamentals are solid and their long-term growth outlooks are very attractive, especially given that they're diversified into areas like real estate and AI infrastructure that are both experiencing solid growth right now.

They pay attractive dividends, have strong balance sheets, and asset-light business models. In the infrastructure space, the Brookfield subsidiaries, like BIP and Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP)(BEPC), as well as well-run, actively managed CEFs like the Reaves Utility Income Fund (UTG) and the Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (UTF), are attractive ways to play AI infrastructure alongside utilities while generating attractive mid- to high single-digit yields along with growth over time. This way, we are not overly dependent on interest rates falling precipitously to drive double-digit annualized total returns.

Energy Producers: Still Finding Value

The energy production segment is hard to find attractive values right now, given that they have rallied so strongly in the wake of the Iran war. Compounders like Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ), Exxon Mobil (XOM), and Chevron (CVX) should be quality picks over the long term, and more energy-sensitive midstream infrastructure names like ONEOK (OKE) could also potentially fit in this basket, especially after its recent dip.

You could also play the inverse of it by betting on industrial stocks that tend to suffer from high energy prices, like United Parcel Service (UPS), as this one should soar higher if energy prices ever move lower. You could combine it with a blue-chip energy producer like CNQ to hedge against energy prices in either direction, all while generating attractive yields and solid long-term growth potential.

Why Precious Metals Leases Beat Mining Stocks

Finally, in the precious metals space (GLD)(SLV), I like investing primarily in gold and silver leases because you own bullion fully, do not pay any management fees, and generate a mid-single-digit yield paid monthly. There are also some mining stock names that look attractive at times, including the likes of Agnico Eagle Mines (AEM), Newmont (NEM), and Barrick Mining (B). However, with these, you have to be picky, and I tend to wait until they sell off pretty aggressively before buying them and view them as trading instruments, whereas my core leasing position is my long-term buy-and-hold precious metals income-generating position.

The Capital Recycling Engine That's Quietly Supercharging My Returns

Within these sectors, I employ opportunistic capital recycling by waiting for picks to come into favor and selling them as they approach fair value, and recycling the capital into other opportunities. Of course, this often is driven by entire sectors moving into favor, as midstream has recently, and gold miners did not too long ago. When that happens, while I may significantly reduce my exposure to a sector, I am always careful to still maintain some exposure in order to maintain adequate portfolio diversification. I then tend to reallocate the proceeds from those sales into a diversified basket of positions, either currently in my portfolio or new ideas that have come along, spread across several sectors to maintain good diversification.

Originally, precious metals were my biggest position by sector; then, when they soared and came into favor last year, I started rotating capital into midstream and energy producers, and that became my biggest position this year. With them soaring higher, I've been gradually reducing my exposure to MLPs and energy stocks and have been pushing the capital into sectors like BDCs, alternative asset managers, and REITs, while still maintaining core positions in energy and precious metals. In fact, energy and precious metals remain my two largest positions, and I also still have some exposure to infrastructure.

Ultimately, no strategy is perfect, and I've gotten plenty of individual and sector names wrong; however, I've been able to get them right far more than wrong. My diversification has helped to keep any of my losers from having too outsized an impact on my portfolio. As a result, I've been able to enjoy significant outperformance over time, and I'm confident I remain positioned to do well, all while generating a high single-digit yield in my portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Opera: AI-Driven Advertising Prospects - Upside Potential And Rich Dividend Yields
data.description:
I previously reiterated my Buy rating for Opera Limited (NASDAQ:OPRA) in December 2025, given the attractive risk/reward profile from the discounted valuations and the rich forward dividends.

Given the ongoing stock price consolidation, OPRA remains a Great Buy here, aided by the management's promising FQ1'26/FY2026 guidance, the robust return prospects across double digits capital appreciation, inflated hedged dividend yields, and expanded shareholder equities from the new $300M share repurchase program.

OPRA Offers Diversified, Profitable Growth Prospects

Since my last Buy rating, OPRA has offered an excellent stock price return of +9.1% compared to the wider market at +0.7%, thanks to the well received FQ4'25 earnings call in late February 2026.

Combined with the rich dividends, the stock has delivered an even richer total return of +12.1%, with it underscoring their compelling dual pronged return prospects as discussed in my last article.

1. FQ1'26 Preview

For now, with OPRA set to report their FQ1'26 performance in April 23, 2026, I urge readers to compare their upcoming performance against the management's previously offered revenue guidance at $170.5M at the midpoint (+19.4% YoY) and adj EBITDA at $39M (+20.7% YoY).

Despite these promising numbers, I want to offer another metric for consideration, ie: their adj EPS numbers, since their recent performance has been rather underwhelming.

For example, OPRA has suffered through:

  • the elevated cost of inventory sold (the cost for third party advertising inventory that is sold to our customers along with our own inventory to better serve our advertisers’ demand) at $205.12M in FY2025 (+72.8% YoY) against
  • the slower growing advertising revenues of $396.04M (+36.2% YoY),
  • with it contributing to the deteriorating gross margins of 64.1% (-8.3 points YoY).

With the management expecting to drive further growth in the Opera Ads platform, I am of the opinion that OPRA's upcoming FQ1'26 adj EPS performance is likely to underwhelm expectations, as similarly observed in the last two bottom-line misses in FQ3'25 and FQ4'25.

Combined with the stagnant adj EBITDA margins at ~23%/24% since FY2023 and the flat guidance entering FY2026, I urge readers to temper their expectations entering the FQ1'26 earnings call.

Otherwise, despite the growth pains from the higher advertising costs, OPRA's robust revenue growth and stable profit margins have contributed to the consensus' promising forward estimates, with the company expected to report an excellent top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +16.2%/+23.5% through FY2028.

This is compared to the historical growth profile at a CAGR of +10.7%/-0.9% between FY2019 levels and FY2025 levels, respectively, with it apparent that the recent stock price underperformance is unwarranted.

2. Robust Advertising/Browser Prospects In An AI World

Beyond the advertising segment's near-term dilutive impact on the bottom-lines, it is apparent that OPRA has been able to rapidly monetize their new e-commerce advertising opportunities, as observed in the high double digits revenue growth in FY2025 and further expansion across the $100B of e-commerce advertising TAM.

Much of their tailwinds are attributed to the growing advertiser partnerships and the higher average spend per advertiser by +56% YoY in FY2025 (for the top 50 advertiser cohort), with it mitigating their stagnant Monthly Active Users [MAU] in the Western regions and in the GX gaming browser.

Particularly, OPRA has demonstrated their ability to directly monetize Opera AI in its browser, as observed in the "200% growth in non-search query revenue," thanks to the direct promotion of its advertising partner "either as a part of the URL experience or in an AI test with Opera AI."

As the management reports further adoption trends of Opera AI in its browsers across its users/advertising partners, it is unsurprising that the management has hinted at the non-search query segment to be their " fastest-growing revenue component in 2026."

Readers may also want to closely monitor the management's commentaries for the Opera Neon's subscription trends, since the Agentic AI browser's public launch on December 11, 2025 may be fully accretive from FQ1'26 onwards.

Moving beyond the browser segment, OPRA is seemingly tapping into the increasingly crowded fintech sector through the prior launch of MiniPay, a stablecoin wallet, embedded into its browser.

This is aided by MiniPay's integration with numerous large partners, including Mercado Libre (MELI) in Latam and numerous banks in the US/the EU.

With the decentralized/expanded fintech approach (paying bills/top up data/P2P remittance, amongst others) already contributing to the higher adoption trends at 13M activated wallets and 390M transactions, as numerous governments also embrace stable coins/cryptocurrencies, it is unsurprising that the management has hinted at their global fintech ambitions ahead.

These underscore OPRA's well diversified monetization capabilities across e-commerce advertising, AI-driven affiliate queries, and even, fintech offerings, with it underscoring their likely to be robust growth prospects beyond the free to download browser and the nascent Neon subscription trends.

3. Compellingly Valued, Profitable Growth Trends

Thanks to the correction from the prior 2025 highs and the YTD consolidation, I am of the opinion that OPRA's bottom-line headwinds have already been baked into its discounted FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 10.90x, down from the 5Y mean of 19.14x and the sector median of 22.31x.

Based on the already discounted FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 10.90x and the consensus adj EPS growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of +23.5%, it is apparent that OPRA remains attractively valued at my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.46x, compared to the wider market at 1.38x.
